votes.reason: The Python code accurately translates the Coq specifications, correctly implementing the vowel/consonant checks, valid position logic, and the specification for finding the closest valid vowel from the end of the string.
The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification, accurately identifying the last valid vowel position surrounded by consonants and checking if the result matches it.
The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification, accurately translating the helper functions and the logic for finding the last valid vowel position.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq spec. Both check if length < 3 returns empty string. For length >= 3, the Coq spec finds a valid vowel position i with no valid vowel after it (i.e., the rightmost valid vowel position), and Python finds this by iterating and keeping the last valid position. The valid_vowel_position logic matches: checking i > 0, i < len-1, vowel at i, consonants at i-1 and i+1. The result comparison (single character string vs empty string) is equivalent.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. Both check if a vowel is surrounded by consonants (not at first or last position). The Coq spec finds the rightmost such vowel using 'no_valid_vowel_after' which means no valid position exists after index i. The Python code iterates forward and keeps updating last_valid_i, effectively finding the rightmost valid position. Both return empty string for words shorter than 3 characters or when no valid vowel position exists. The Python returns the single character at that position, matching the Coq's 'String ch EmptyString'.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. Both check if a vowel is surrounded by consonants (not at first or last position), and both find the rightmost such vowel. The Coq spec uses 'no_valid_vowel_after' to identify the last valid position, while Python iterates and keeps updating last_valid_i, achieving the same result. For strings with length < 3, both return empty string. The helper functions (is_vowel, is_consonant, valid_vowel_position) produce identical results on concrete inputs.
Both select the last index where a vowel is flanked by non-vowels and return that single-character result, or empty if none; edge cases (length < 3) and bounds match.

Both define vowels identically, consider any non-vowel a consonant, and select the last index where a vowel has consonant neighbors; otherwise they require the empty string. Index bounds and iteration match Coq’s spec, so results coincide on all concrete inputs.

Both select the last index i (1 <= i <= len-2) where the character is a vowel and both neighbors are non-vowels, returning that single character, and return empty string if length < 3 or no such position exists. Helper predicates and indexing behavior align with the Coq spec for all concrete ASCII inputs.
